title: Voiced and voiceless consonants
summary: Use original human recordings to hear final devoicing and voicing changes in Czech consonant clusters.
objectives: Distinguish voiced and voiceless consonant pairs
Recognize voicing assimilation in consonant clusters
Use TTS only as a fallback when a recording cannot play

sections.heading: Voiced and voiceless pairs
sections.body: Czech uses the pairs b–p, v–f, d–t, z–s, ď–ť, ž–š, g–k, and h–ch.

sections.heading: Final devoicing
sections.body: A voiced consonant at the end of a Czech word is normally pronounced as its voiceless partner.

sections.heading: Consonant clusters
sections.body: Within a cluster, consonants commonly assimilate to the voicing of the final consonant in that cluster.

vocabulary.note: Final d is pronounced like t.
Final ž is pronounced like š.
The cluster changes voicing in normal pronunciation.
Initial v is pronounced like f before the following voiceless consonants.

exercises.prompt: How is the final consonant in led normally pronounced?
exercises.explanation: Final voiced d is devoiced to t.

exercises.prompt: Listen to the synthesized fallback and choose the spelling.
exercises.explanation: The spelling is muž. Use the human recording above as the primary pronunciation model.

exercises.prompt: Type the Czech word for joke.
